top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: Conflict still dominates. Russia hit Ukraine with one of the war's largest missile-and-drone barrages overnight, with Ukrainian officials now reporting at least 17 dead and more than 100 hurt across Kyiv and Dnipro. In the Gulf, Iran suspended talks with Washington and a Guard commander declared 'war is inevitable' without a deal, even as Trump insisted negotiations continue; a US-announced Israel–Hezbollah truce is already fraying, with clashes overnight. Away from the fighting, Anthropic filed confidentially for a near-$1-trillion US IPO and a trial drug nearly doubled survival in advanced pancreatic cancer.

stories.headline: One of war's largest Russian barrages kills at least 17 in Ukraine
stories.summary: Ukraine's Air Force said Russia fired 73 missiles and 656 attack drones at Kyiv, Dnipro and other cities overnight into 2 June — among the largest combined barrages of the war — of which 642 were shot down or suppressed, while 38 locations were struck. President Zelensky and the State Emergency Service reported deaths in both Dnipro and Kyiv, putting the overall toll at at least 17 killed and more than 100 injured, with rescue work continuing (Ukrainian figures; toll still being updated).

stories.headline: Iran suspends US talks as Guard commander says 'war is inevitable'; Trump insists deal is close
stories.summary: Iranian state media said Tehran suspended talks with Washington in protest at Israel's expanding offensive in Lebanon, and a senior Revolutionary Guard officer said 'war is inevitable' without an agreement and that Iran 'will never surrender.' President Trump countered that negotiations were 'continuing at a rapid pace.' The US said its Strait of Hormuz blockade had redirected 121 commercial vessels and disabled five, while Iran said it had supervised the passage of 15 vessels; Defence Secretary Hegseth said the US was 'more than capable' of resuming strikes (each side's account).

stories.headline: US-announced Israel–Hezbollah truce frays as the two sides clash overnight
stories.summary: President Trump said Israel and Hezbollah had agreed to halt hostilities — with no Israeli troops entering Beirut and Hezbollah ceasing fire — and Lebanon said it accepted a framework to be widened to 'all Lebanese territories.' But the two sides clashed overnight, the Israeli military said it intercepted projectiles from Lebanon, and PM Netanyahu said the IDF would 'continue to operate as planned' in the south and would hit Beirut if attacks persisted. Lebanon said it would seek to expand the truce in Washington talks on 3 June (claims attributed to the parties).

stories.headline: Anthropic confidentially files for US IPO at a valuation near $1 trillion
stories.summary: AI company Anthropic said it has confidentially submitted a draft S-1 registration statement to the US Securities and Exchange Commission for a proposed initial public offering; the number of shares and the price range have not been set, per TechCrunch. The filing follows a reported $65 billion funding round that lifted its valuation to roughly $965 billion. If it debuts near $1 trillion it would rank among the largest US IPOs and put Anthropic ahead of rival OpenAI in going public.

stories.headline: Experimental drug nearly doubles survival in advanced pancreatic cancer in phase 3 trial
stories.summary: Phase 3 results presented at the American Society of Clinical Oncology meeting and published in the New England Journal of Medicine showed the experimental RAS inhibitor daraxonrasib roughly doubled median overall survival — to 13.2 months versus about 6.7 months with chemotherapy — in patients with previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer, with fewer serious side effects. The RASolute 302 trial enrolled about 500 patients; the drug, made by Revolution Medicines, is not yet FDA-approved but the data will support a regulatory submission.

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: A quiet local evening, with weather and travel the main threads. The NEA forecast issued at 5.40pm is partly cloudy for the rest of today and fair and warm tomorrow, with highs of 35°C and a high chance of afternoon showers. The one disruption to watch: Singapore Airlines retimed at least 14 flights between Singapore and Japan on 2–3 June as Typhoon Jangmi bears down on Japan, and warns more flights could be affected.

stories.headline: NEA: Partly cloudy today, fair and warm tomorrow, up to 35°C
stories.summary: The National Environment Agency's 24-hour forecast, updated at 5.40pm on 2 June, expects partly cloudy conditions for the rest of the day and fair, warm weather on 3 June, with a 55–80% chance of showers. Temperatures are forecast between 27°C and 35°C, with south-southeasterly winds of 10–25 km/h. The outlook fits a broader warmer, drier trend forecasters have flagged for the coming months, which raises the risk of transboundary haze later in the year.

stories.headline: SIA retimes at least 14 Singapore–Japan flights as Typhoon Jangmi nears
stories.summary: Singapore Airlines said it would reschedule at least 14 flights to and from Japan on 2 and 3 June as Typhoon Jangmi approached the country, affecting services to Osaka, Nagoya and Tokyo's Haneda and Narita airports. Several Singapore departures have been pushed to 3 June; SIA said it would contact affected customers and warned that, with the situation 'fluid,' other flights may be affected. The storm has also disrupted air and rail travel within Japan.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: Escalation across the board. Russia hit Ukraine with one of the war's largest barrages overnight, with Ukraine reporting at least 17 dead in Kyiv and Dnipro. In the Gulf, Iran suspended talks and a Guard commander said 'war is inevitable,' while the US kept up its Hormuz blockade and warned it could resume strikes. A US-announced Israel–Hezbollah truce is shaky — the two clashed overnight and Netanyahu vowed to keep operating in south Lebanon — and Gaza's ceasefire still holds only tenuously.

theatres.name: Russia–Ukraine War
theatres.status: Russia struck Kyiv, Dnipro and other cities overnight into 2 June with one of its largest combined missile-and-drone barrages of the war (Ukraine reports at least 17 killed); Ukraine keeps up long-range strikes on Russian oil and energy sites.

timeline.event: Russia launched a large overnight combined attack on Kyiv; Ukraine's Air Force said roughly 600 drones and 90 missiles were used, including a hypersonic Oreshnik. Mayor Klitschko reported deaths and dozens injured with damage across the capital (Ukrainian figures).

Ukraine's military said it struck the Saratov oil refinery and other Russian energy and industrial targets overnight; regional governors in several Russian regions reported drone strikes. Ukraine denied a Russian claim that it had targeted the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant (Ukraine's military; Russian regional officials).

Ukraine's General Staff reported about 229 combat clashes over the prior day, with heavy fighting in the Pokrovsk sector; Russian drones were reported to have hit Kharkiv and Odesa, injuring civilians, while Ukraine continued long-range strikes on Russian oil facilities (Ukrainian figures, not independently verified).

Ukraine's Air Force said Russia fired 73 missiles and 656 drones at Kyiv, Dnipro and other cities overnight — one of the war's largest barrages — of which 642 were downed or suppressed while 38 locations were hit. President Zelensky and the State Emergency Service reported at least 17 killed and more than 100 injured across Kyiv and Dnipro (Ukrainian figures; toll still being updated).

theatres.name: US/Israel–Iran (Strait of Hormuz)
theatres.status: Tehran suspended talks with the US on 1 June over Israel's offensive in Lebanon; a Revolutionary Guard commander said 'war is inevitable' without a deal, while Trump insisted talks continue and the US kept up its Hormuz blockade and warned it could resume strikes.

timeline.event: After Iran talks failed, Trump announced the US would impose a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Reporting indicated the US and Iran had discussed a draft deal to extend the ceasefire about 60 days, reopen Hormuz shipping, end the US blockade and begin nuclear talks (draft contents attributed to sources; not a finalised agreement).
Trump ended an Iran deal meeting without announcing a 'final determination,' leaving negotiations unresolved.

US and Iranian forces traded strikes: the US said it hit command sites at Qeshm Island and Goruk in self-defence; Iran's IRGC said it struck a US-used air base in Kuwait, which US Central Command said it intercepted (two ballistic missiles, no casualties). Kuwait said its air defences engaged 'hostile missiles and drones' (each side's account).

Iranian state media said Tehran had suspended talks with Washington over Israel's offensive in Lebanon, and a senior Revolutionary Guard officer said 'war is inevitable' without a deal and that Iran 'will never surrender.' Trump said talks were 'continuing at a rapid pace.' The US said its blockade had redirected 121 commercial vessels and disabled five, while Iran said it had supervised 15 vessels through the strait; Defence Secretary Hegseth said the US was 'more than capable' of resuming strikes (each side's account; full closure remains a stated threat, not a confirmed action).

theatres.name: Israel–Lebanon (Hezbollah)
theatres.status: A US-announced partial ceasefire (Israel to halt strikes on Beirut, Hezbollah to stop attacks) is fragile: the two sides clashed overnight into 2 June, Israel said it intercepted projectiles from Lebanon, and Netanyahu vowed to keep operating in the south.

timeline.event: A US-brokered Israel–Lebanon ceasefire took effect and was later extended, but Hezbollah disarmament remained unresolved and both sides traded blame for violations.

Israeli ground forces pushed past the Litani River, described as the deepest Israeli incursion into Lebanon in more than 25 years; Al Jazeera reported Israel holding roughly 2,000 sq km of Lebanese territory (figures attributed to the parties / Al Jazeera).

A partial ceasefire was announced: Israel to refrain from strikes on Beirut and Hezbollah's southern suburbs, Hezbollah to halt attacks on Israel, per Lebanese officials and President Trump. Lebanon said attacks nonetheless continued and that it would seek to widen the truce in Washington talks; Netanyahu said Israel would keep operating in southern Lebanon (announcement and claims attributed to the parties).

Despite the announced truce, Israel and Hezbollah clashed overnight; the Israeli military said it intercepted two projectiles fired from Lebanon into northern Israel, with no injuries, and Netanyahu said the IDF would 'continue to operate as planned' in the south and would strike Beirut if attacks persisted. Lebanon said it would seek to expand the ceasefire to all Lebanese territory in Washington talks on 3 June (claims attributed to the parties; origin of the projectiles not independently confirmed).

theatres.name: Israel–Gaza
theatres.status: The October ceasefire holds tenuously but phase-two talks remain stalled over Hamas disarmament; Israel continues periodic strikes it calls responses to threats, and Iran cited Israeli operations in Gaza in suspending US talks.

timeline.event: Board of Peace envoy Mladenov said the Gaza ceasefire hinges on Hamas disarmament, calling Hamas's obligation to give up its arsenal 'not negotiable'; phase-two progress remained blocked.

Human Rights Watch said Israel was curbing aid and killing civilians during the ceasefire, with aid volumes remaining below UN-stated minimums and nearly the whole population still displaced and aid-dependent (HRW findings; Israel says aid is flowing with coordination continuing).

Al Jazeera reported on Israel's long-term plans for Gaza amid the tenuous truce; local medical sources said hundreds of Palestinians had been killed since the truce began, which Israel disputes, saying strikes targeted threats to its forces (casualty totals attributed to Gaza medical sources, not independently verified).

Iran's IRGC cited Israeli military operations in Gaza (alongside Lebanon) as justification for threatening shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and Bab al-Mandeb, underscoring that the stalled Gaza truce remains a driver of wider regional escalation (Iranian statements).

outbreaks.label: Outbreaks
outbreaks.ai_summary: The dominant, escalating threat is the Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic in DR Congo and Uganda, a WHO-declared PHEIC: confirmed cases are around 291 with about 43 deaths (Africa CDC and the DRC health ministry, late May/1 June), on top of more than 1,100 suspected cases and an exported case being treated in Germany. There is no licensed vaccine or cure for this Ebola species. Two slower-moving outbreaks round out the picture: a person-to-person Andes hantavirus cruise-ship cluster (13 cases, 3 deaths), now largely contained, and elevated US measles at 1,983 cases with no deaths.

outbreaks.name: DRC/Uganda Bundibugyo Ebola (PHEIC)
outbreaks.status: Rapidly escalating; PHEIC since 17 May; no licensed Bundibugyo-specific vaccine or therapeutic
outbreaks.scope_note: Laboratory-confirmed cases/deaths, DRC + Uganda combined: DRC 282 confirmed / 42 deaths (DRC Ministry of Health, as of 31 May) plus Uganda 9 confirmed / 1 death (≈291 / 43). Africa CDC's 1 June briefing gave a combined 263 confirmed / 43 deaths as of 30 May, with more than 1,100 suspected cases still under investigation (NOT added to the confirmed totals). WHO's verified line list (DON605, 29 May) trails at 134 confirmed / 18 deaths — a reporting lag, not a confirmed-vs-suspected mismatch. Ituri province is the epicentre; a separately-counted exported case (a US doctor infected in DRC) is being treated in Germany.

outbreaks.name: MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster (multi-country)
outbreaks.status: Largely contained; contacts monitored across 32 countries during the long incubation window
outbreaks.scope_note: Cases linked to the cruise ship MV Hondius: 11 confirmed + 2 probable = 13, with 3 deaths (2 confirmed, 1 probable; CFR ~23%), per WHO Disease Outbreak News DON604 (as of 27 May), matching ECDC. Confirmed cases reported in 4 countries (Canada, Netherlands, Spain, US); more than 600 contacts across 32 countries/territories are under monitoring but are NOT counted as cases. Andes virus is the only hantavirus with documented person-to-person spread; WHO rates the global risk low.

outbreaks.name: United States measles
outbreaks.status: Elevated and outbreak-driven; no deaths reported in 2026
outbreaks.scope_note: Confirmed US measles cases in 2026 across 40 jurisdictions, per the US CDC as of 28 May; 0 deaths reported in 2026. About 93% (1,847) are outbreak-associated across 30 outbreaks. US-only figure; part of a wider global measles resurgence.

stories.headline: Bundibugyo Ebola in DRC and Uganda nears 300 confirmed cases as PHEIC response strains
stories.summary: Confirmed Bundibugyo virus cases have climbed sharply: the DRC Ministry of Health (31 May) put DRC at 282 confirmed and 42 deaths, which with Uganda's 9 confirmed and 1 death gives a combined total of about 291 confirmed and 43 deaths. Africa CDC's 1 June briefing logged a combined 263 confirmed and 43 deaths as of 30 May, plus more than 1,100 suspected cases still under investigation. WHO's Disease Outbreak News DON605 (29 May) recorded a lower 134 confirmed and 18 deaths, its verified line list trailing the national tallies. The outbreak — a PHEIC since 17 May, with Ituri province the epicentre — has no licensed vaccine or treatment for this Ebola species.
stories.source: Africa CDC / DRC Ministry of Health / WHO DON605

stories.headline: Ebola case exported to Europe as a US doctor infected in DRC is treated in Germany
stories.summary: A US medical doctor infected with Bundibugyo virus while treating patients in DRC is receiving care in Germany, per Africa CDC and UN News. No onward transmission has been reported outside DRC and Uganda. The exported case is counted separately from the DRC and Uganda totals.

stories.headline: Cruise-ship Andes hantavirus cluster holds at 13 cases, 3 deaths as contacts monitored across 32 countries
stories.summary: WHO's Disease Outbreak News DON604 reports the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster at 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) and 3 deaths as of 27 May, with confirmed cases in Canada, the Netherlands, Spain and the US. More than 600 contacts across 32 countries and territories are under monitoring given the virus's long incubation period. Andes virus is the only hantavirus known to spread person-to-person; WHO rates the global risk low.

stories.headline: US measles tops 1,980 cases for 2026, almost all outbreak-linked
stories.summary: CDC data as of 28 May show 1,983 confirmed US measles cases in 2026 across 40 jurisdictions and 30 outbreaks, with 93% of cases outbreak-associated and no deaths reported this year. The total reflects sustained, interconnected outbreaks amid a wider global measles resurgence.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: Active exploitation is the theme this cycle. Google's June Android update fixes a zero-day (CVE-2025-48595) already used in targeted attacks, and CISA added a two-year-old Oracle WebLogic flaw (CVE-2024-21182) to its must-patch list after seeing it exploited in the wild. The most severe single bug remains the CVSS 9.8 Windows Netlogon RCE (CVE-2026-41089), now hitting domain controllers. Rounding out the week: a supply-chain compromise of Red Hat npm packages spreading credential-stealing malware, and an actively exploited Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN bypass.

stories.headline: Google patches actively exploited Android zero-day among 124 June flaws
stories.summary: Google's June 2026 Android security update fixes 124 vulnerabilities, including CVE-2025-48595, which Google says shows indications of 'limited, targeted exploitation.' The flaw lets a local attacker execute code and escalate privileges on devices running Android 14 or later. Google did not name a threat actor, but similar privilege-escalation bugs have previously been abused by commercial spyware vendors and nation-state operators against high-value targets.

stories.headline: CISA flags two-year-old Oracle WebLogic flaw as actively exploited
stories.summary: CISA added CVE-2024-21182, a high-severity Oracle WebLogic Server flaw originally patched in July 2024, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation. The remotely exploitable bug lets an unauthenticated attacker with network access compromise the server and gain access to its data, affecting versions 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.1.0.0. Federal agencies were ordered to patch by 4 June, with private organisations urged to do the same.

stories.headline: Critical Windows Netlogon RCE flaw now actively exploited against domain controllers
stories.summary: A stack-based buffer overflow in Windows Netlogon lets unauthenticated attackers run remote code on domain controllers across all supported Windows Server versions, including Server 2025. Microsoft patched it in the May 2026 Patch Tuesday, but Belgium's national cybersecurity centre (CCB) and others now report active exploitation in the wild and urge immediate patching. Given Netlogon's role in domain authentication, successful exploitation can compromise an entire Active Directory environment.

stories.headline: Red Hat npm packages compromised to spread credential-stealing malware
stories.summary: More than 30 npm packages under Red Hat's '@redhat-cloud-services' namespace (about 96 versions, ~117,000 weekly downloads) were compromised to distribute a Shai-Hulud-style credential stealer dubbed 'Miasma,' which harvests GitHub Actions secrets, AWS and other cloud credentials, SSH keys and CI/CD tokens. Attackers compromised a Red Hat employee's GitHub account to publish the backdoored versions. Red Hat said the compromise was limited to internal development and that no malicious code reached customers via console.redhat.com.

stories.headline: Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN auth-bypass flaw now exploited in attacks
stories.summary: Palo Alto Networks confirmed limited exploitation of CVE-2026-0257, an authentication-bypass flaw in GlobalProtect that lets attackers establish an unauthorised VPN connection without valid credentials; the severity was raised from medium to high once attacks were observed. Rapid7 reported successful exploitation across multiple customers, with attackers forging authentication-override cookies to target administrator accounts. Exploitation requires specific configurations (override cookies enabled and a particular certificate setup).
